title: Faithfulness vs Fluency
concept: The tension between a strictly grounded, sometimes-terse answer and a fluent, sometimes-embellished one.

exercises.prompt: When building a grounded QA system, what is the best way to handle a situation where the retrieved documents do not contain the answer?

options.text: Have the model search the public internet to guess the answer anyway.
Instruct the model to say 'I don't know' if the answer isn't in the provided text.
Force the model to choose the closest matching word even if it is incorrect.

exercises.explain: In the previous lesson, we learned that when the context is missing the answer, the model should output a standard 'I don't know' fallback message rather than guessing.

cards.title: The Golden Tongue Dilemma
cards.body: When we build AI apps, we want answers that are both perfectly accurate and beautiful to read. But here is the catch: the more we push an AI to write a smooth, elegant story, the more likely it is to make up details just to sound good.

cards.title: Understanding the Trade-off
cards.body: Faithfulness is how closely an AI stick to the provided facts without inventing details. Fluency is how smooth, natural, and readable the AI's response is. Often, making a response sound super polished and flowy can lead the AI to slip in ungrounded embellishments.

cards.title: The Stenographer vs. The Novelist
cards.body: Think of Faithfulness as a strict court stenographer who only writes down facts verified by the witnesses. Fluency is a charismatic novelist who wants the story to sound beautiful, flow naturally, and keep readers hooked.

cards.title: Comparing the Outputs
cards.body: Given the text 'Product launched in 2021', a faithful but less fluent response is: 'Launch year: 2021.' A highly fluent but less faithful response is: 'We proudly debuted our groundbreaking product line in 2021 to instant acclaim.' (Notice the ungrounded praise!)

cards.title: The Eloquence Trap
cards.body: Many believe that a well-written, articulate response must be correct. In reality, LLMs are trained to write beautifully, which makes their plausible-sounding hallucinations even harder to spot if we don't enforce strict grounding.

cards.title: The Balancing Act
cards.body: Let's look at how pushing for one side can hurt the other. Choosing the right balance depends on whether you are writing creative copy or extracting clinical data.

left.text: 'Patient age: 45. History: None.' (Dry, disjointed, but completely safe and true to source)

right.text: 'The patient is a healthy 45-year-old individual showing an excellent medical history.' (Flows well, but adds ungrounded assumptions)

exercises.prompt: In the context of LLM evaluation, what does 'Fluency' specifically measure?

options.text: How strictly the generated text aligns with the provided source documents.
How natural, readable, and grammatically correct the generated text sounds.
The speed at which the model generates tokens.
The model's ability to admit when it does not know the answer.

exercises.explain: Fluency focuses on the language structure, grammar, and natural flow of the generated text, regardless of whether the facts are accurate.

exercises.prompt: True or False: If a generated answer sounds highly fluent and professional, it is guaranteed to be faithful to the retrieved context.
exercises.explain: A response can be perfectly fluent (grammatically flawless, beautiful flow) while containing 100% fabricated facts (completely unfaithful).

exercises.prompt: Fill in the blank to complete the explanation of why highly fluent models might hallucinate details.
exercises.sentence: If you tell a model to write a highly engaging and smooth response, it is more likely to embellish details that are not in the source.
exercises.explain: When we prioritize fluency, the model tends to embellish or add extra stylistic words that aren't supported by the retrieved document.

exercises.prompt: Review the facts: 'Founded 2012. 50 workers.' Spot the response that sacrificed faithfulness for flashy, ungrounded fluency.

options.text: Facts: Founded 2012. 50 workers. Answer: The company was founded in 2012 and has 50 workers.
Facts: Founded 2012. 50 workers. Answer: Founded in 2012, this company employs a team of 50 people.
Facts: Founded 2012. 50 workers. Answer: Established in 2012, this highly advanced firm operates a state-of-the-art facility with 50 dedicated workers.

exercises.explain: Option C is the AI-generated one because it adds ungrounded marketing fluff ('state-of-the-art facility', 'highly advanced') that is completely absent from the source facts. Option A is faithful but choppy, and Option B is faithful and moderately fluent.

exercises.prompt: You are building a critical medical search assistant where factual accuracy is life-or-death, but the retrieved text is highly technical and choppy. Which prompt strategy best manages the tension by prioritizing extreme faithfulness over fluency?

options.text: Rewrite the retrieved context in a poetic, engaging style to make it easier to read.
Instruct the model to write in a literal, dry style using only direct facts from the source.
Ask the model to generate a long, polite introductory paragraph before answering.
Allow the model to guess the missing details to make the narrative flow better.

exercises.explain: To prioritize strict faithfulness above all else, we should instruct the model to use the exact words from the source and avoid adding any extra adjectives or connecting details that aren't explicitly proven by the text. Let the system prompt command a literal style.
